coredata.dc:title: Boron and nitrogen co-doped bright yellow fluorescent carbon dots as real-time selective detection of phthalic acid plasticizer in aqueous medium
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Ashokan, Indhumathi
Bhunia, Susanta Kumar
coredata.dc:description: Excessive usage and discharge of phthalic acid in the environment affect endocrine system of living beings via bioaccumulation in food chain. In this paper, boron and nitrogen co-doped yellow fluorescent carbon dots (B, N-C-dots) have been designed for the rapid recognition of plasticizer phthalic acid. These C-dots were prepared by carbonization of 4-bromo-1,2-diaminobenzene and boric acid mixture. The bright fluorescence (PLQY = 26.4 %) of C-dots showed a significant Stokes shift of 145 nm under 430 nm excitation wavelength. These B, N-C-dots selectively detect phthalic acid in aqueous solution instantaneously and experienced a maximum 95 % fluorescence quenching immediately upon the treatment with phthalic acid with a limit of detection of 91.12 nM. It has been proved that the combined effect of strong intermolecular hydrogen bonding, chemical conjugation, and non-fluorescent ground state complex formation between the probe and analyte led to fluorescence quenching of the C-dots. Static quenching has been confirmed from changes in B, N-C-dots UV–vis absorbance spectroscopy and non-changes in fluorescence lifetime after adding phthalic acid. Furthermore, these B, N-C-dots were successfully applied to recover the analyte in real water samples and showed an average recovery rate of 96.4–102 % with a good relative standard deviation from spiked samples. This study provides selective recognition ability in the field of environmental monitoring and remediation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: B, N-C-dots
Plasticizers
Phthalic acid selective detection
Fluorescence quenching
Detection mechanism

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of B, N-C-dots

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Indhumathi A. acknowledges VIT University for providing fellowship and seed money as research grant. S. K. Bhunia expresses his appreciation to “VIT SEED GRANT” and DST INSPIRE funding (DST/ INSPIRE Faculty Award/2018/DST/INSPIRE/04/2018/002484) and SERB (SRG/2020/000645) for financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–vis absorbance (black line), excitation (red broken line), and emission (yellow line) spectra of B, N-C-dots. (B) Emission spectra under mentioned excitation wavelengths. (C) TEM image and (D) size histogram of B, N-C-dots.
(A–D) Deconvoluted XPS spectra of C 1s, O 1s, N 1s, and B 1s show different functional moieties on the B, N-C-dots surface. (E) FT-IR spectrum of the synthesized B, N-C-dots.
(A) Structures of plasticizers. (B) Changes of B, N-C-dots’ fluorescence intensity upon the treatment with plasticizers. (C) Fluorescence images of B, N-C-dots under 365 nm UV lamp illumination.

(A) Changes of B, N-C-dots’ emission intensity against various concentrations of PA between 0.37 µM and 145 µM. (B) Fluorescence digital images of B, N-C-dots treated with PA at the mentioned concentrations.

(A) Quenching mechanism of B, N-C-dots with the addition of PA. (B) FT-IR spectra of B, N-C-dots (i) before and (ii) after addition of PA. (C) Modulation of UV–vis absorbance spectra of C-dots in the presence of analytes. (D) Fluorescence decay of the Yellow C-dots before and after the addition of PA.
An overall representation for the synthesis of yellow fluorescent B, N-C-dots and selective detection of phthalic acid.
Recovery rates of PA in real water samples.

head.ce:title: Boron and nitrogen co-doped bright yellow fluorescent carbon dots as real-time selective detection of phthalic acid plasticizer in aqueous medium

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, School of Advanced Sciences, Vellore Institute of Technology, Vellore 632014, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, School of Advanced Sciences, Vellore Institute of Technology, Vellore 632014, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Excessive usage and discharge of phthalic acid in the environment affect endocrine system of living beings via bioaccumulation in food chain. In this paper, boron and nitrogen co-doped yellow fluorescent carbon dots (B, N-C-dots) have been designed for the rapid recognition of plasticizer phthalic acid. These C-dots were prepared by carbonization of 4-bromo-1,2-diaminobenzene and boric acid mixture. The bright fluorescence (PLQY = 26.4 %) of C-dots showed a significant Stokes shift of 145 nm under 430 nm excitation wavelength. These B, N-C-dots selectively detect phthalic acid in aqueous solution instantaneously and experienced a maximum 95 % fluorescence quenching immediately upon the treatment with phthalic acid with a limit of detection of 91.12 nM. It has been proved that the combined effect of strong intermolecular hydrogen bonding, chemical conjugation, and non-fluorescent ground state complex formation between the probe and analyte led to fluorescence quenching of the C-dots. Static quenching has been confirmed from changes in B, N-C-dots UV–vis absorbance spectroscopy and non-changes in fluorescence lifetime after adding phthalic acid. Furthermore, these B, N-C-dots were successfully applied to recover the analyte in real water samples and showed an average recovery rate of 96.4–102 % with a good relative standard deviation from spiked samples. This study provides selective recognition ability in the field of environmental monitoring and remediation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Exponential development of chemical industries and high consumption of daily lives release various types of industrial wastes in the environment. Some of the wastes are carcinogenic and affect the endocrine system [1]. These compounds are called endocrine disruptors. They mimic the biological activity of hormone system and lead to different reproductive problems [2]. One of the colorless plasticizers named phthalic acid is an example of endocrine disruptors and is a major source in plastic industry [3]. It is used for the synthesis of different phthalate compounds, also known as endocrine disruptors. Their presence in water causes deformity, infertility, miscarriages, gene mutation, and cancer upon ingestion even at low concentration [4,5]. Therefore, it is very essential to selectively detect phthalic acid in wastewater. Traditional analytical methods such as high performance liquid chromatography, gas chromatography mass spectrometry, atomic absorption spectroscopy, etc. have been reported to detect such type of plasticizer [6,7]. Although, some techniques are capable to detect at lower concentrations, but usage of sophisticated instruments, time-consuming, complicated sampling preparations, skilled operator requirement limit their frequent usage and onsite detection [2,5]. On the other hand, fluorometric method has aroused significant attention due to its fast analysis time, low cost, easy and simplicity [8]. Thus, the fabrication of fluorophore for selective recognition of phthalic acid is highly desirable.

Carbon dots (C-dots) have proved their competitive potentiality compared other nanomaterials because of small size (<10 nm), tunable fluorescence, simple preparation procedure, excellent biocompatibility, chemical stability, etc [9,10]. Their fluorescence properties are used to design any type of sensor such as biomolecule, drug, metal ions, pollutants, etc [11,12]. Their wide ranges of available precursors give prior preference in the list of cost-effective nanomaterials. In-built formation of functional groups on the sp2 graphitic carbon core of C-dots surface during synthesis extends the solubility in different solvents and track analytes easily in solution [13]. The interaction between C-dots and analytes results in the modulation of fluorescence properties that can be explained by quenching, recovery, electron/energy transfer, etc. There are plenty of literatures where C-dots have been applied to sense different type of metal ions, bioactive matters, enzymes, some bacterials, pharmaceutical drugs, etc [14,15]. However, the detection number of colorless plasticizers using C-dots is sparse [16] and especially phthalic acid has not been investigated by C-dots.

With this aim, we developed highly selective fluorescent B, N-C-dots via simple carbonization technique for the instantaneous detection of phthalic acid. A mixture of 4-bromo-1,2-diaminobenzene and boric acid was used as raw materials to synthesize yellow fluorescent B, N-C-dots with the fluorescence quantum yield value of 26.4 %. Investigation of spectroscopic and microscopic properties reveals the C-dots formation in association with surface functional groups. The fluorescence intensity of C-dots’ aqueous solution underwent an immediate dramatic decrease with the incubation of phthalic acid. This sensor performed well with a linear concentration range of 0.37 µM-145 µM with a limit of detection of 91.12 nM. Although, incubation with other competent plasticizers showed complete silent nature even at higher concentration. Strong intermolecular hydrogen bonding interaction between carboxylic group of phthalic acid and B-OH and amine functional moieties on the C-dots’ surface and chemical conjugation could result in selective fluorescence quenching. In addition, static quenching type mechanism was also proved via observed changes of UV–vis absorbance spectroscopy and unchanged nature of B, N-C-dots’ lifetime. Real water sample analysis revealed almost complete recovery of the analyte with a good relative standard deviation.

4-Bromo-1,2-diaminobenzene and dimethyl acetamide (DMA) were purchased from Avra synthesis Pvt. ltd. Boric acid, phthalic acid, dibutyl phthalate, and silica gel were bought from Sisco Research Laboratories Pvt. ltd. Dioctyl phthalate was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Triphenyl phosphate, ethyl benzoate, and tricresyl phosphate were purchased from High Purity Laboratory Chemicals Pvt. ltd. Ethanol was bought from CSS reagent.

Carbonization process was chosen to prepare yellow emissive B, N-C-dots. Briefly, 100 mg of 4-bromo-1,2-diaminobenzene and boric acid were dissolved in 3 mL of DMA and the mixture was heated at 140 °C for 5 h. The consequent brown-colored solution was purified via column chromatography using ethanol as a mobile phase to remove the unreacted molecules. Next, ethanol was removed by rotary evaporation to obtain a crude product. Afterward, deionized water was added to the product and the solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 15 min. Finally, the supernatant was collected and used for further experiments.

The morphology of B, N-C-dots was investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) using a 200 kV JEOL JEM-2100F microscope. Jasco V-730 spectrophotometer was used to measure UV–vis absorbance spectra. Fluorescence measurement was carried out in Synergy H1-H1M multimode microplate reader. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was carried out by SHIMADZU IRAffinity-1 spectrophotometer. The pH of the solutions was measured by a digital pH meter (Systronics India ltd.). Horiba Yobin’s (DeltaFlex-01) time-correlated single-photon counting spectrometer was used for the measurement of C-dots lifetime. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured by the ESCA+ omicron nanotechnology oxford instrument.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of B, N-C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: A simple carbonization technique was selected to synthesize B, N-C-dots from a mixture of 4-bromo-1,2-diaminobenzene and boric acid in presence of dimethyl acetamide as a solvent. The mixture was heated at 140 °C for 5 h and a brown black color solution was obtained. The resulting C-dots solution was purified via column chromatography in ethanol as solvent. Ethanolic solution was evaporated to obtain solid powder and deionized water was added into it. The C-dots water dispersion showed a bright yellow fluorescence under portable UV lamp excitation (λex = 365 nm). The brightness was dramatically turned-off with the incubation of phthalic acid. Although, other plasticizers showed complete ineffective nature upon their incubation. An overall representation has been shown in Scheme 1 . Fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of these B, N-C-dots was measured and calculated as 26.4 % with respect to fluorescein dye as standard. The formation of bright yellow fluorescent B, N-C-dots was assumed from the contribution of carbon core, ratio of sp2 over sp3, and defect sites. Carbon core was formed from the carbonization of 4-bromo-1,2-diaminobenzene at 140 °C temperature. The longer time heating duration helped in nucleation of the carbon core and resulting in higher extent of conjugation. These two combined effects increased the ratio of sp2 and sp3 carbons and introduced very bright yellow fluorescent B, N-C-dots [8].

In addition, doping of hetero atoms such as nitrogen and boron from respective carbon precursor and boric acid imparts defect sites in the carbon matrix. This also affects the fluorescence properties of C-dots. Individual carbonization of 4-bromo-1,2-diaminobenzene also led to yellow fluorescence (Fig. S1). But, the brightness was very low and fluorescence QY was reported as 4.0 %. Surprisingly, the addition of boric acid implanted a very bright fluorescence of formed C-dots indicating its necessity for high quality C-dots synthesis. The doping of boron atoms in the carbon matrix introduces p-type carriers and defect sites that change their photoluminescence properties. Additionally, vacant p-orbital of boron atom participate in conjugation with sp2 hybridized carbon and reduce the distance between sp2 cluster and the dopant. This may lead to an increase in electron delocalization and very high radiative recombination rate resulting in enhanced fluorescence quantum yield [17–20]. The prepared B, N-C-dots were characterized by spectroscopic and microscopic analysis. UV–vis absorbance spectrum of these C-dots solution revealed two prominent peaks at 290 nm and 430 nm corresponding for π-π* and n-π* transition respectively (Fig. 1 A) [8]. These transitions arise from aromatic constituents and functional groups associated with C-dots surface. A maximum intensity of photoluminescence excitation spectrum positioned at 430 nm as shown in Fig. 1A. The highest emission intensity was recorded at 575 nm under 430 nm excitation wavelength. Emission spectra of B, N-C-dots were measured at different excitation wavelengths ranging between 400 and 500 nm with an interval of 50 nm and showed excitation independent emission properties (Fig. 1B). It indicates the relative size uniformity and lean surface energy states distribution of C-dot particles [15,21]. TEM study in Fig. 1C revealed small sized particles. Size distribution histogram were represented in Fig. 1D and showed an average diameter of 2.0 ± 0.6 nm.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was analyzed to check the functional groups and chemical bonding on the B, N-C-dots surface. The survey spectrum in Fig. S2 confirmed the attendance of carbon, oxygen, nitrogen, and boron. Fig. 2 shows the deconvoluted spectra of those elements. The two peaks of C 1s deconvoluted spectra were positioned at 284.2 eV and 285.8 eV. These corresponded for CC/CC and CN bonds respectively [22]. The O 1s deconvoluted spectra showed two peaks at 531.6 eV and 532.6 eV for respective CO and OC-OH and/or C-OH groups [23]. The deconvoluted peaks of N 1s at 398.4 eV and 399.6 eV were represented for pyridinic nitrogen and CN and/or CN groups respectively [24,25]. The peaks of B 1s centered at 184.5 eV and 191.5 eV were assigned to B-C and B-O groups respectively [26]. FT-IR spectrum also revealed different functional groups on the C-dots surface. The peaks appeared at 3360 and 3190 cm−1 corresponded for OH and NH stretching vibrations (Fig. 2E) [24,27]. The B-OH peaks showed in the region of 2930, 1215, and 1026 cm−1 [28,29]. The bending vibration of NH was shown in 1516 cm−1. The peak at 1400 cm−1 corresponded for OH in plane deformation [18]. The peak at 796 cm−1 represented to B-O-B respectively. This confirms the presence of boron and nitrogen-containing functional groups on the C-dots’ surface. XRD spectrum in Fig. S3 showed a sharp peak at 2θ value of 25° and ascribed to (002) graphite plane, propounding disorganization pattern of C-dots because of the insertion of nitrogen, oxygen, and boron-containing functional groups. All of these spectroscopic techniques confirmed the implantation of boron and nitrogen into the C-dots matrix. The stability of C-dots was measured at different pH. Aqueous dispersion of these C-dots was mixed with different pH ranging from 3 to 12. From Fig. S4, it was observed that C-dots emission intensity is comparatively lower in acidic pH and the emission intensity becomes higher in neutral and basic pH. The lower in fluorescence intensity was due to the protonation of surface functional amine groups at low pH. The fluorescence becomes restored in neutral and basic condition due to the deprotonation of the amine groups. Thus, fluorescence property of these B, N-C-dots can be used in neutral and basic medium. The photostability of these synthesized C-dots was examined by irradiating UV lamp of 150 W up to 1 h with different interval. Aliquots were taken at different intervals and emission spectra were measured. Fluorescence images and spectra of the B, N-C-dots aliquotes were collected and shown in Fig. S5. The result showed no significant loss of C-dots fluorescence intensity and proved good photostability.

The applicability of the synthesized B, N-C-dots was explored towards the detection of plasticizers. A known concentration (145 µM) of plasticizers namely phthalic acid (PA), tricresyl phosphate (TCP), triphenyl phosphate (TPP), dibutyl phthalate (DBP), ethyl benzoate (EB), dioctyl phthalate (DOP) were mixed with C-dots’ aqueous dispersion separately. The structure of these compounds has been represented in Fig. 3 A. C-dots’ fluorescence property was measured instantaneously by recording emission spectra and capturing digital images (Fig. 3B-C). Emission spectra were collected by irradiating an excitation wavelength of 430 nm and emission intensity of C-dots at 575 nm was compared after the treatment with the mentioned plasticizers. It was observed that emission intensity of B, N-C-dots was surprisingly decreased to approximate 95 % after the treatment with PA. Contrastingly, other analytes showed almost negligible effect. Notably, they showed around 5–8 % emission enhancement. Fluorescence digital images of the C-dots illuminated at 365 nm excitation wavelength in Fig. 3C carried the same signature as that of emission spectra.

Next, various concentrations ranging between 0.37 µM and 145 µM of PA solution were added to the C-dots aqueous dispersion to measure the sensibility and stability. Fig. 4 A represents the variation of B, N-C-dots fluorescence intensity against various concentrations of PA. It is evident that the emission intensity at 575 nm wavelength gradually decreased with increase in the incubation of PA. A maximum quenching efficiency of 95 % was recorded with 145 µM PA treatment. Fluorescence digital images were also captured after the treatment with mentioned PA concentrations and shown in Fig. 4B. The brightness of C-dots’ emission gradually attenuated with increase in PA and no fluorescence was observed at 145 µM. The changes of emission brightness completely corroborate with fluorescence spectra of C-dots in Fig. 4A. Normal daylight images of these C-dots were also captured after the treatment with PA different concentrations. The color changes were clearly observed even at low concentration of PA as shown in Fig. S6. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated by plotting the variation of intensity against PA concentration. The value was reported as 91.12 nM from a linear graph with an excellent correlation coefficient R2 of 0.9997 (Fig. S7). The calculated LOD value is very significant and comparable with recommended levels [30–32]. A comparative study for multifarious plasticizers detection using different materials through various techniques has been presented in Table S1. Notably, this comparison has been studied with respect to phthalate compounds as there is no article related to direct PA detection and PA is used to prepare phthalate compounds. Quenching constant was also measured from Stern-Volmer equation to check the extent of binding and the value was calculated as 2.3x105 M−1. Such strong value indicates the strong interaction among PA with C-dots’ surface functional groups [15].

To prove the selectivity of these C-dots towards PA, interference studies were performed. First, aqueous suspension of B, N-C-dots was mixed individually with negative plasticizers (TCP, TPP, DBP, EB, and DOP) and fluorescence intensity was measured. Next, PA was added to the mixtures and emission spectra were further measured. Fig. S8 reflects the significant decrease in emission intensity of C-dots after addition of PA. It confirms the activeness of these C-dots selectivity in presence of other plasticizers. Furthermore, C-dots solution was added to the mixture of all non-quenching analytes together and fluorescence was measured. No changes were observed with respect to bare C-dots as shown in Fig. S9. Next, PA was added into the C-dots-negative analytes mixture and the measured spectrum showed around 95 % decrement of C-dots fluorescence intensity. This study further confirms the B, N-C-dots selectivity towards PA ample the presence of plasticizers mixture.

The quenching of B, N-C-dots’ fluorescence upon the treatment with PA could be ascribed to the hydrogen bonding, ester bond formation, and electron donor–acceptor properties. The presence of different functional groups including boron, nitrogen, and oxygen on B, N-C-dots’ surface were proved from FT-IR and XPS studies. Strong intermolecular hydrogen bonding between carboxylic acid groups of PA and functional groups on C-dots’ surface assists to bring small molecular weight analytes in close proximity of the fluorophore (Fig. 5 A). The presence of B-OH (in majority) and amine functional groups take part in hydrogen bonding. Thus, the delocalization of π-electrons through such hydrogen bonding stabilizes the ring parameters. Formation of hydrogen bonding was proved from FT-IR spectroscopy. A very broad peak was observed in between 3600 cm−1 and 2200 cm−1 after the addition of PA with C-dots as shown in Fig. 5B. It confirms the intermolecular hydrogen bonding of carboxylic acids of PA with hydroxyl and amine groups of C-dots. Furthermore, intensities of B-OH peaks at 1026 cm−1 and 1189 cm−1 decreased to a certain extent and a significant new peak appeared at 1710 cm−1. It indicates the formation of ester bond due to the condensation reaction between hydroxyl group of B-OH and carboxylic group of PA [33]. Notably, very less quenching (∼8%) was observed upon the treatment of PA with C-dots prepared from only 4-bromo-1,2-diaminobenzene due to the lower extent of hydrogen bonding between amine and carboxylic groups (Fig. S10). This confirms that mostly B-OH groups associate in interaction with PA and these B-OH groups come only from boric acid. It emphasizes the importance of boric acid during C-dots synthesis for detection of PA. Static quenching was proposed and proved behind the B, N-C-dots’ fluorescence decrement. A certain concentration of the C-dots aqueous solution was mixed individually with different plasticizers and UV–vis absorbance spectra were measured. It is very clear from Fig. 5C that there was a significant difference of C-dots absorbance before and after the addition of PA. A bathochromic shift of the absorbance peak (from 430 nm to 465 nm) was observed indicating the extended conjugation with PA. Whereas, no changes were noticed for the non-quenching analytes. In addition, a significant increase in absorbance intensity was also observed in case of B, N-C-dots and PA mixture. These indicate the complex formation between the fluorophore and the analyte [34,35]. Fluorescence lifetime was also measured to know the reason behind. The values were calculated as 1.16 ns and 1.08 ns before and after PA treatment respectively with good χ2 values of 1.07 and 1.09. The decay kinetics has been represented in Fig. 5D. It showed there were no changes in B, N-C-dots fluorescence decay and good fitting was also observed. The changes of absorbance spectrum and unchanged behavior of fluorescence lifetime of C-dots confirm the static quenching responsible for quenching of fluorescence [8]. The static quenching happens due to the formation of a stable non-fluorescent ground state complex between the quencher and fluorophore. To further prove the static quenching, the UV–vis absorbance spectrum of B, N-C-dots was measured after the addition of PA at different temperatures which has been shown in Fig S11. The gradual decrease of absorbance intensity was observed with increase in temperature starting from 20 °C to 60 °C. This phenomenon further adds the involvement of static quenching [35]. An aggressive increment nature of Stern-Volmer plot (Fig. S12) and the good binding constant value also support the static quenching.

To investigate the practical applicability of these B, N-C-dots, the proposed method was petitioned to detect PA in three kinds of water samples including drinking water, industrial water, and river water. First, the water samples were filtered through a syringe filter to remove large particles of diameter 0.45 µm. A definite amount of PA was spiked with the filtered water samples premixed with C-dots and fluorescence was recorded immediately. The results were analyzed and presented in Table 1 . The recovery rate was reported in between 96.4 % and 102 % for all the water samples after adding PA with a good relative standard deviation. The other constituents in real water samples did not affect to recognize PA in detection medium. This result proves the potentiality of these B, N-C-dots to detect PA in real field application.

In summary, a novel fluorescent sensor has been developed for the selective detection of phthalic acid which is widely used as plasticizer. Yellow fluorescent B, N-C-dots have been synthesized from a mixture of 4-bromo-1,2-diaminobenzene and boric acid via simple carbonization approach. The small sized particles were formed with a maximum fluorescence QY of 26.4 %. Decoration of boron, nitrogen, and oxygen associated functional groups into the surface of C-dots was proved by spectroscopic techniques. These fluorescent probes were used to detect PA selectively. Bright fluorescence intensity was immediately attenuated upon the addition of PA to these C-dots aqueous solution. The concentrations between 0.37 µM and 145 µM were detected with a maximum quenching efficiency of 95 %. Limit of detection value was reported as 91.12 nM. A combined effect of strong intermolecular hydrogen bonding, chemical conjugation and static quenching was reported as responsible quenching factor. As a further matter, this proposed method was used for the quantification of PA in real water sample analysis and showed a satisfactory recovery results. Therefore, these B, N-C-dots could provide an excellent fluorescent probe to recognize plasticizers in aqueous solution to sort out water pollution.

Indhumathi Ashokan: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Data curation, Conceptualization. Susanta Kumar Bhunia: Conceptualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Indhumathi A. acknowledges VIT University for providing fellowship and seed money as research grant. S. K. Bhunia expresses his appreciation to “VIT SEED GRANT” and DST INSPIRE funding (DST/ INSPIRE Faculty Award/2018/DST/INSPIRE/04/2018/002484) and SERB (SRG/2020/000645) for financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2022.114489.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

Talanta

ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces

Analyst

ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots as an effective fluorescent sensing platform for metal ion detection
Nanoscale Res. Lett.
